primary_diagnosis.name: Critical limb ischaemia (chronic limb-threatening ischaemia) due to severe peripheral arterial disease

pathophysiology_summary: Peripheral arterial disease is atherosclerotic narrowing of the limb arteries. It progresses from intermittent claudication (exertional muscle ischaemia relieved by rest) to critical limb ischaemia (chronic limb-threatening ischaemia) when resting perfusion is inadequate: ischaemic REST pain, typically in the toes/forefoot, is worst when supine and at night (loss of the gravity-assisted perfusion and reduced cardiac output/blood pressure during sleep) and is characteristically relieved by hanging the leg over the side of the bed (dependency), because gravity augments the marginal arterial inflow. Examination shows the signs of severe flow limitation: diminished/absent distal pulses, a cool, pale, shiny, hairless limb with delayed capillary refill, and Buerger's test positive - elevation pallor (the leg blanches when raised) followed by dependent rubor (a dusky red flush when lowered, from reactive hyperaemia in a maximally dilated ischaemic bed) - with an ankle-brachial index that is severely reduced (typically <0.4 in rest pain). Trophic changes and, later, ulceration/gangrene ensue; this is a limb-threatening emergency requiring urgent vascular assessment and revascularisation. It must be distinguished from ACUTE limb ischaemia (a sudden cold, pale, pulseless, painful, paraesthetic, paralysed limb - the 6 Ps - typically embolic, a surgical emergency within hours), from diabetic neuropathy (sensory, without the macrovascular signs), and from DVT (a swollen, warm, erythematous limb). Smoking is the dominant modifiable driver and an acute vasoconstrictor. Management is urgent vascular assessment (ABI/imaging) and revascularisation to save the limb, antiplatelet and statin therapy, effective analgesia, and absolute smoking cessation. Pulses, ABI and safe strong analgesia cannot be delivered over telehealth, so the correct action is urgent same-day vascular/emergency assessment, with immediate emergency care if acute limb ischaemia develops.

differential.evidence_basis: Ischaemic rest pain in the toes relieved by dependency, on a background of claudication, is classic critical limb ischaemia.
Must be excluded/safety-netted, though the chronic claudication history and positional relief (rather than a sudden cold, pulseless, paralysed limb) favour critical rather than acute ischaemia.
Can cause foot pain, but the macrovascular signs (absent pulses, dependent rubor, elevation pallor) point to flow limitation, not isolated neuropathy.
A cool, pale, pulseless foot is not the swollen, warm, erythematous limb of DVT.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The AI Doctor should recognise critical limb ischaemia - a limb-threatening emergency - and arrange urgent same-day vascular/ED assessment, with acute-limb-ischaemia safety-netting.

differential.evidence_basis: Ischaemic rest pain relieved by dependency (DI-001) evolving from claudication (DI-002), with (sealed) absent distal pulses, elevation pallor and dependent rubor and trophic changes (DI-003), and no acute-limb features (DI-004), is critical limb ischaemia.
Remains the key thing to safety-net; if the foot suddenly becomes cold/pale/numb/paralysed it is an immediate surgical emergency.
The macrovascular signs indicate flow limitation, not isolated neuropathy.
A cool, pale, pulseless limb is not DVT.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After the red-flag screen this is critical limb ischaemia: urgent same-day vascular/ED assessment for imaging/ABI and revascularisation, antiplatelet, effective analgesia and absolute smoking cessation, with acute-limb-ischaemia safety-netting.

differential.diagnosis: Critical limb ischaemia due to severe PAD - requiring urgent vascular assessment/revascularisation
differential.evidence_basis: Working diagnosis for telehealth. Pulses/ABI/imaging and safe analgesia require in-person assessment; the correct action is urgent same-day vascular/ED assessment to try to save the limb.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Final telehealth framing is critical limb ischaemia (limb-threatening) needing urgent same-day vascular/ED assessment - not a remote opioid script or a routine referral, and immediate emergency care for acute limb ischaemia.

red_flags.description: Ischaemic rest pain in the toes relieved by dependency (critical limb ischaemia)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Rest pain relieved by hanging the leg down indicates critically inadequate resting perfusion - a limb-threatening state.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Progression to ulceration, gangrene and limb loss without urgent revascularisation.

red_flags.description: Progression from claudication to rest pain (worsening critical ischaemia)
red_flags.clinical_significance: The transition from exertional to rest pain marks progression to critical limb ischaemia requiring urgent action.

red_flags.description: Acute limb ischaemia features (sudden cold/white/blue/black, numb or paralysed foot - the 6 Ps) - ABSENT but must be safety-netted
red_flags.clinical_significance: These would indicate acute limb ischaemia, an immediate surgical emergency within hours; their absence supports urgent (same-day) rather than immediate-minutes escalation, but they must be safety-netted.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: If acute limb ischaemia develops and is not acted on immediately, the limb is lost.

red_flags.description: Absent distal pulses, elevation pallor with dependent rubor (positive Buerger's test), delayed capillary refill (>4 s), and early atrophic toe changes on clinician examination; ABI severely reduced (<0.4)
red_flags.clinical_significance: These clinician-elicited signs and the ABI confirm severe flow limitation/critical ischaemia; they cannot be obtained over telehealth.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Not applicable to telehealth - requires clinician examination/ABI; the correct action is urgent in-person vascular assessment.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Treating critical limb ischaemia as ordinary claudication
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Rest pain relieved by dependency is limb-threatening, not the stable claudication of earlier PAD; it demands urgent vascular assessment.
Analgesia is needed but does not address the limb threat; the priority is urgent vascular assessment to try to save the limb.
The AI Doctor must screen for and safety-net a sudden cold/pale/numb/paralysed foot, which is an immediate surgical emergency.
Continued smoking is an acute vasoconstrictor worsening perfusion; immediate cessation is a limb-saving action.

risk_scores.expected_score_or_tier: Severely reduced ABI (anticipated <0.4) consistent with critical limb ischaemia
risk_scores.clinical_implication: A severely reduced ABI confirms critical ischaemia and the need for urgent revascularisation; cannot be measured over telehealth.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Palpation of distal pulses and Buerger's test (elevation pallor/dependent rubor)
Capillary refill and limb temperature/viability assessment
Ankle-brachial index and vascular imaging (duplex/CT/MR angiography)
Antiplatelet initiation with assessment of contraindications
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Urgent same-day in-person vascular assessment (a vascular surgery service or the emergency department) for pulses/ABI/imaging, effective analgesia and consideration of revascularisation; advise absolute immediate smoking cessation, and keep the leg dependent for comfort - with IMMEDIATE emergency department attendance if the foot turns white, blue or black, or becomes numb or paralysed (acute limb ischaemia).
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Prescribing painkillers remotely and arranging a routine referral, or dismissing this as ordinary claudication, risks progression to gangrene and limb loss, and misses acute limb ischaemia.
